title: Login password sent as SHA-256 hash without server challenge (pass-the-hash)
description: The client sends wired.user.password = SHA256(plaintext) in wired.send_login. If an attacker captured this hash, they could replay it directly at the application layer. The P7 key exchange ECDSA proof was also derived from SHA256(plain), meaning a hash from the DB was sufficient to forge the key exchange.
recommendation: Bind the ECDSA key exchange proof to a value that requires knowledge of the plaintext. Add a per-user server-side salt so SHA256(plain) alone is insufficient to authenticate.
patch_description: P7 v1.2 (commit f806993) adds a per-user stored_salt (DB column password_salt). The server sends it encrypted to the client during key exchange (server_challenge message). The client derives base_hash = SHA256(stored_salt || SHA256(plain)). An attacker with only SHA256(plain) from the DB cannot produce a valid ECDSA proof without also knowing stored_salt, which is never stored client-side. The wired.send_login message still carries SHA256(plain) but is transmitted inside the encrypted P7 tunnel after key exchange succeeds — an attacker must break the session encryption to intercept it. The original exfiltration vector (FINDING_A_003, hash leak) is also patched.
